coredata.dc:title: In situ growth of 1T-MoS2 nanosheets on NiS porous-hollow microspheres for high-performance potassium-ion storage

Qiu, Youjie

An, Jiye
Liu, Chunting
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a two-step route was adopted to in situ grow 1T-MoS2 nanosheets on NiS porous-hollow microspheres. It was found that the tiled MoS2 sheets tightly wrapped NiS spheres through coherent attachment via lattice-plane rotation, which would fully activate inert sulfur atoms on the basal plane of 1T-MoS2. This unique structure resembled a concentric spherical capacitor, which was able to store a large quantity of K+ ions with a capacity of 273.1 mAh/g at 5 A/g and deliver a stable capacity of 472.1 mAhg−1 after 1000 cycles at 0.2 A/g. In these multilevel hollow spheres, NiS phase mainly acted as a dielectric material to boost K-storage capability of MoS2 lamellae, and this electrical double-layer effect is more prominent either at high current densities or upon long-term cycling. It was anticipated that this work would shed new light on designing heterostructures of transition-metal dichalcogenides for high-performance potassium-ion storage.

head.ce:title: In situ growth of 1T-MoS2 nanosheets on NiS porous-hollow microspheres for high-performance potassium-ion storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a two-step route was adopted to in situ grow 1T-MoS2 nanosheets on NiS porous-hollow microspheres. It was found that the tiled MoS2 sheets tightly wrapped NiS spheres through coherent attachment via lattice-plane rotation, which would fully activate inert sulfur atoms on the basal plane of 1T-MoS2. This unique structure resembled a concentric spherical capacitor, which was able to store a large quantity of K+ ions with a capacity of 273.1 mAh/g at 5 A/g and deliver a stable capacity of 472.1 mAhg−1 after 1000 cycles at 0.2 A/g. In these multilevel hollow spheres, NiS phase mainly acted as a dielectric material to boost K-storage capability of MoS2 lamellae, and this electrical double-layer effect is more prominent either at high current densities or upon long-term cycling. It was anticipated that this work would shed new light on designing heterostructures of transition-metal dichalcogenides for high-performance potassium-ion storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Global decarbonization is now pushing for great advancements in affordable and reliable battery technologies for energy storage [1]. In this industrial context, abundance of battery materials is probably the first and foremost requirement for realization of battery applications in building electrical power grids, and in this respect, the most popular lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are in straitened circumstances. Even in the field of portable power, the supply of LIBs is becoming increasingly difficult to meet the rapidly growing battery demand due to scarce and unevenly distributed lithium and cobalt resources on earth. Given these mentioned above, potassium-ion batteries (PIBs) stand out from multiple alternatives to LIBs and find ever-expanding roles in large-scale applications such as stationary energy storage. The advantages of PIBs are mainly illustrated through inherent characteristics of potassium, including but not limited to (i) rich and evenly-distributed reserves [2]; (ii) low redox potential [3]; (iii) small Stokes radius after solvation due to its weak Lewis acidity [4,5] and (iv) low desolvation energy [6]. Nevertheless, just like the cow that gives a good pail of milk and then kicks it over, its demerit of large radius (∼1.38 Å) is also prominent and always troublesome [7]. Constrained by this drawback, the diffusion kinetics of K+ within electrode materials is rather sluggish, even though its migration in carbonate-based electrolytes is fairly fast. On the whole, this will result in poor rate performance of the battery, which makes it virtually impossible to apply in high-power apparatuses such as electric vehicles. Moreover, repeated insertion and deinsertion of big K+ ions will cause poor structural stability of electrode materials. This is very detrimental to cycle performance of the battery, which appears as pronounced polarization and rapid capacity decay [8–12].

To mitigate such ill effects on battery performance, structural optimization of electrode materials should be a key part of the design cycle. Developing new structures of electrode materials with enhanced K-storage capability using simple and versatile methods has been an ever-demanding aspect in PIBs technology. Among the structures of all dimensions, lamellar structure has always been highly anticipated by research community, in that it not only provides two-dimensional channels for K+ insertion and adequate room for K+ accumulation but also presents considerable surface area for K+ adsorption. As a typical representative of layered materials, molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) has been widely studied mainly because of its four-electron transfer character in (electro) chemical reactions and abundant active sites [13,14]. It can exist in two allotropic forms, including metallic octahedral 1T phase (1T-MoS2, Oh symmetry) and semi-conductive trigonal prismatic 2H phase (2H-MoS2, D3h symmetry) [15,16]. Given highly populated states at the Fermi level, 1T-MoS2 is capable of efficiently transporting and storing charges, enabling its bright future in the field of energy storage [17]. Recently, maturing hydro (solvo) thermal techniques in synthesizing 1T-MoS2 have promoted its practical applications to a new level [18,19]. Despite the progress achieved to date, the (electro) chemical activity of 1T-MoS2 has not lived up to expectations, seeing that sulfur atoms on the basal plane are in a near thermodynamic equilibrium state. In view of this, several strategies that are based on electronic regulation have been proposed to activate these inert sulfur atoms, among which constructing heterostructures is most popular [20,21].

Usually, such heterostructures can be easily established by modifying hydro (solvo) thermal routes to the synthesis of MoS2, which can be roughly divided into two categories. The first is one-pot route, which is conducted by adding precursors of both main phase (i.e. 1T-MoS2) and heterophase in a whole [22]. On account of immense discrepancies in nucleation conditions and growth rates of different phases, the resultant heterostructures are prone to phase separation, so that the electronic structure of 1T-MoS2 cannot be effectively modulated. The second is two-step route, which is conducted by grafting heterophase onto pre-synthesized 1T-MoS2 main phase [23]. Unfortunately, the posterior synthesis of heterophase often causes the conversion of 1T-MoS2 into 2H-MoS2, thereby significantly reducing electronic conductivity of final structure. Moreover, their phase boundaries are often less than satisfactory. Some of the resultant structures mainly consist of heterophase core and 1T-MoS2 shell, wherein the shell is vertically distributed around the periphery of the core [24–26]. Other structures are built from randomly scattered heterophase particles onto 1T-MoS2 lamellae [27–30]. Regardless of morphological structures, their phase boundaries usually exhibit “point-contact” feature, being incapable of full activation on sulfur atoms.

To tackle this interface issue, we reverse the synthetic order of the two-step route. In this study, porous-hollow NiS microspheres are firstly synthesized, and then they serve as scaffold to in situ grow interlacing 1T-MoS2 nanosheets. The resultant structure looks somewhat like calyx covering the young buds of flowers. In this structure, “surface contact” between NiS and 1T-MoS2 can efficiently activate inert sulfur atoms on the basal plane of 1T-MoS2. It is also conducive to the penetration of electrolyte, and hence facilitating the accessibility to K+. Besides that, it offers short diffusion distance for charge transport and fast interlaminar charge collection. Moreover, it brings about physical robustness which is propitious to buffering volume change upon (de)potassiation. To demonstrate these structural superiorities, electrochemical experiments (including galvanostatic test, cyclic voltammetry test and electrochemical impedance test) are systematically carried out, and thereby the K-storage mechanism is fully discussed. As our structural design is independent of individual character of the element, it can be generalized to other combinations of transition-metal dichalcogenides, so that the heterostructures for K-storage will be greatly broadened.
All chemicals were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China) and used directly without further refinement.

The synthetic route included two steps. In the first step, NiS hollow spheres (named as SNiS) were synthesized via a hydrothermal procedure that was reported elsewhere [31]. In the second step, 0.6 g NiS powder and 1.4 g absolute ethanol were mixed together to form colloid. Then, 10 mL aqueous solution containing 150 mg sodium molybdate and 200 mg thiourea was added. After stirring with a glass rod for 30 min, the semisolid substance was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 20 mL for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 18 h. The target product (named as SNiS/MoS2) was repeatedly washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol, and finally dried in air at 60 °C overnight. Besides, pure MoS2 (named as SMoS2) was prepared by merely performing the second synthetic step without the addition of ethanol solution of NiS. For comparison, a hybrid of SNiS and SMoS2 with a weight ratio of 6:4 was also prepared and named as Shybrid. This weight ratio is set according to the measured atomic ratio of Ni2+ and Mo4+ in SNiS/MoS2. Therefore, Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2 have nearly identical phase content so as to ensure comparability of their electrochemical performance.

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/max-rA) was used to determine phase composition of samples. A Super LabRam microscopic Raman spectrometer (532 nm He-Ne laser, Jobin Yvon) was used to provide structural information of samples. A Quantachrome Instrument (NOVA 1000e) was used to measure nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of samples. A BRUKER TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer was used to measure Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometry of samples. A VG ESCALAB 220i-XL system was used to collect X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) of samples. An inductively coupled plasma-optic emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Perkin-Elmer Optima 2000) was used to detect ion concentrations of Ni2+ and Mo4+ in SNiS/MoS2. A field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL JSM-6700F) was used to showcase morphologies of samples. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2010 TEM, 200 kV) was used to reveal microstructures of samples. A Field Emission Gun microscope (JEOL 2010F) with a point resolution of 0.19 nm was used to take high-resolution transmission electron microscopies (HRTEM) of samples.

To prepare working electrode for a half-cell, a hybrid of active material (70 wt%), acetylene black (20 wt%) and polyvinylidene fluoride (10 wt%) was fully ground and dispersed into a proper amount of N-methyl pyrrolidone to form a black slurry. A piece of copper foil was used as current collector, onto which the slurry was scraped with a mass loading of ∼ 1.0 mg cm−2. After vacuum drying, the foil was stamped under 20 MPa to make circular working electrodes.

Then, CR2032-type coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box (Etelux, Lab2000) with fresh potassium slices and polypropylene film (Celgard 2500) as the reference electrode and separator, respectively. The electrolyte was prepared by dissolving potassium hexafluorophosphate (KPF6) into equal volume of ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) with a concentration of 0.8 mol/L.

Neware battery cyclers (CT-4008 T-5 V10/50 mA-164) were used to test cycle performance of cells at various current densities. An electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) was used to measure cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves in a voltage range of 0 ∼ 3 V as well as electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) in a frequency range of 10 mHz ∼ 100 kHz. Voltages mentioned in this work were referred to a K/K+ redox couple and the presented specific capacities were calculated on the basis of active-material mass.

Further, several tested cells were disassembled in the argon-filled glove box and their working electrodes were exfoliated completely and rinsed successively with acetonitrile and absolute ethanol before further characterization.

Fig. 1 a shows XRD patterns of Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2, in which the peak intensity of the latter is substantially lower than that of the former. Such weakness of diffraction peaks confirms a highly defective nature of SNiS/MoS2, which is caused by the corrosive effect of thiourea in the second synthetic step [32,33]. Comparatively, the crystallinity of MoS2 phase is much poorer than that of NiS phase, and this situation is more prominent in the case of SNiS/MoS2. The characteristic peaks at 14.1°, 32.8° and 49.4° can be assigned to (002), (100) and (105) planes of MoS2 according to the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) File (No. 75–1539). Notably, the strongest (103) peak in the standard diffraction card becomes less conspicuous confirming the presence of 1T-MoS2 in both samples [32,34–36]. Besides, a new diffraction peak that belongs to 1T phase appears at 8.7°, indicating the formation of a new lamellar structure with an increasing interplanar spacing compared to that of 2H phase [28,37]. Collectively, in situ growing MoS2 on NiS scaffold can significantly reduce crystallinity of each phase and alter MoS2 phase configuration.

To provide more structural information, Raman scattering experiments are carried out. In Fig. 1b, the collected Raman spectra of SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid mainly show two sets of MoS2 signals that correspond to vibrational modes of S–Mo–S bonds. The characteristic peaks that are located at 372.8 and 401.1 cm−1 can be assigned to in-plane E2g and out-of-plane A1g of 2H phase, respectively [38,39]. This observation powerfully proves the existence of 2H-MoS2 in both samples. Moreover, the distance between E2g and A1g modes reaches 28.3 cm−1, manifesting few-layer feature of the existed 2H phase [28,40]. In terms of SNiS/MoS2, four additional peaks appear on its spectrum, which center around 171.9, 214.0, 281.7 and 339.3 cm−1 confirming the presence of 1T-MoS2 [30,41–44]. Comparatively, these four peaks are less distinctive in the spectrum of Shybrid, revealing NiS phase can promote 2H-to-1T phase transition.

The pore structure of SNiS/MoS2 can be nicely demonstrated by comparative nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments. Fig. 1c and d show that the measured isotherms of SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid bear striking similarities. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, both isotherms can be classified to type IV, revealing the presence of mesoporous structures [45]. Moreover, their H3-type loops clearly manifest the existence of ink-bottle pores with narrow necks and wide bodies [46]. When P/P0 is below 0.8, the adsorption uptakes slightly increase with the increase of P/P0, indicating the existence of micropores. When P/P0 is beyond 0.8, the adsorption uptakes increase remarkably and do not level off, indicating the coexistence of mesopores and macropores. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area is then determined to be 72.9 and 68.1 m2 g−1 for Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2, respectively.

The pore structures of SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid can be further demonstrated by Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore diameter distributions. In Fig. 1e and f, both SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid exhibit a wide pore diameter distribution from 15 to 500 nm, with the most probable diameters around 126 nm for Shybrid and 98 nm for SNiS/MoS2. The BJH cumulative pore volume is estimated to be 0.19 and 0.15 cm3 g−1 for Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2, respectively. Note that both the pore volume and specific surface area of SNiS/MoS2 are apparently smaller than those of Shybrid. Accordingly, it is conjectured that the in situ grown MoS2 nanosheets may well fully cover the NiS scaffold through a surface-contact manner. In this conjecture, the encapsulated NiS phase occupies the internal space built by interconnected MoS2 nanosheets, and thus lowering the pore volume as well as specific surface area of the whole structure.

SEM characterization is then conducted so as to confirm the above speculation and the result is shown in Fig. 2 a. It is evident that SNiS/MoS2 consists of spherical particles with obvious surface adhesions. Their average diameter is 440 nm, which is about 10% smaller than that of SNiS (Fig. S1a). A close-up image in Fig. 2b shows every particle is covered with curving and interlacing MoS2 nanosheets, and hence causing interparticle adhesions. Through the ultrathin MoS2 layer, it can be seen that the surface of inner NiS particles is smooth and dense. On the contrary, SNiS is fairly rough in surface texture which, upon closer scrutiny, reveals a disordered assembly of branched nanosheets with open voids (Fig. S1b). This discrepancy clearly manifests the in situ growth of MoS2 on NiS can induce interface reconstruction, which guarantees adequate surface contact between the two phases and facilitates the activation of inert sulfur atoms on the basal plane of 1T-MoS2. Given that MoS2 phase is prone to homogeneously nucleate and spontaneously grow into flower-like architectures (Fig. S1c and d), it is concluded that NiS spheres not only act as heterogeneous nucleation centers for the birth of MoS2 but also serve as scaffold to direct its growth.

To further illustrate the morphological structure of SNiS/MoS2, TEM characterization is conducted and the result is shown in Fig. 2c. It is seen that spherical particles with a mean diameter of 440 nm are hollow inside and they are apt to extrude each other and conglutinate to gobbet. From a zoom-in view of an individual particle (Fig. 2d), it can be measured that their average shell thickness is 65 nm. Accordingly, the diameter of internal cavity is about 310 nm. By comparison, the spherical particles of SNiS exhibit much better dispersity (Fig. S2a). The mild particle aggregation should be ascribed to slow solvent evaporation during TEM sample preparation. The mean diameters of NiS spheres and internal cavities are 480 and 430 nm, respectively, indicating a shell thickness of 25 nm on average (Fig. S2b). In general, the size of internal cavities shrinks about 28% and the shell thickness expands about 160% after the in situ growth of MoS2. Such remarkable size change is highly relevant to the interface reconstruction. Furthermore, a zoom-in view in Fig. 2e shows the particle surface of SNiS/MoS2 is built from tiled MoS2 nanosheets, which are tightly contacted with NiS particles. The coexistence of NiS and MoS2 phases can be validated by selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2f), in which the spotty rings are caused by insufficient number of diffraction grains. By using the camera constant, the SAED pattern is labeled with Miller indices, which are in line with the aforementioned XRD analysis. Fig. 2g presents a typical HRTEM image that is taken from the marginal region of a particle. The different contrast is due to the different electron penetration efficiency on MoS2 layers with different heights. The atomic lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 1.84 Å correspond to (105) lattice plane of MoS2. Of note, the parallel lattice fringes are unable to extend in the field of view, implying a random distribution of MoS2 nanosheets.

To clearly reveal the characteristics of phase boundary between NiS and MoS2, SNiS/MoS2 is sliced before further characterization. Firstly, SNiS/MoS2 is mixed with the polymer resin and solidified at 80 °C in a vacuum oven overnight. Then the concretion is cut into slices with a uniform thickness of 60 nm using ultramicrotome (PowerTomeXL). After that, HRTEM characterization is carried out to showcase lattice fringes around phase boundaries. Fig. 3 a and b separately show two boundary regions, in which NiS and MoS2 nanocrystals seem to be organized into an epitaxial structure through sharing identical lattice planes. The interplanar spacing of NiS is 2.51 Å corresponding to its (021) crystal plane, while that of MoS2 is 2.28 Å corresponding to (103) lattice plane. By plotting inverse fast Fourier transformation (FFT) patterns, one can observe that the epitaxial interfaces are not iso-oriented (Fig. 3c and d). Based on the lattice parameters of NiS and MoS2 as well as the formula for interplanar spacing of hexagonal structures, one can calculate the angle between (021) plane of NiS and (103) plane of MoS2. The calculated 53° is in excellent consistency with those measured in Fig. 3c and d. That means, although atomic arrangements in NiS and MoS2 lattices differ significantly, they can coherently connect together through crystal-plane rotation. Such rotation model is schematically depicted in Fig. 3e, in which colored balls represent atomic locations in crystal lattice. Under the premise of a rotation of 53°, the green balls at the phase boundary extend from both the (021) plane of NiS and (103) plane of MoS2. In other words, the atoms on the above two crystal planes completely overlap at the interface, and thereby enabling the phase boundary to be of epitaxial feature. This interface structure ensures the maximally exposed MoS2 edges on NiS termination, which is fairly propitious to the activation of sulfur atoms at the MoS2 side [47].

The large contact area between NiS and MoS2 phases in SNiS/MoS2 is conducive to full activation on sulfur atoms of the latter, thereby improving electrochemical performance on the whole. To test this, galvanostatic discharge–charge measurements are performed on SNiS/MoS2 and contrast experiments are conducted on SNiS, SMoS2 and Shybrid. Fig. 4 a depicts the first five voltage curves for different cells measured at 0.2 A/g. It is clearly observed that the shapes of these curves are quite similar because all tested active materials belong to chalcogenides. Notably, the first discharge curve of each sample differs significantly from the rest ones, indicating the presence of irreversible reactions upon initial potassiation. The pseudo plateaux in the range of 1.9 ∼ 2.1 V (discharge) and 2.1 ∼ 2.4 V (charge) should be attributed to reduction and oxidation of electrolyte, respectively. Besides, the plateaux around 0.1 V is caused by the (de)intercalation of K+ into layered graphite (i.e. acetylene black). Except for these two types of platforms, no other ones are discernible in voltage profiles. This finding strongly manifests the absence of conversion reactions related to nickel and molybdenum ions. In Fig. 4a, the first discharge capacity of SNiS is observed to be the highest (983.8 mAhg−1), followed by Shybrid (886.0 mAhg−1), SMoS2 (883.8 mAhg−1), and then SNiS/MoS2 (762.1 mAhg−1). The initial Coulombic efficiency (ICE) that can be expressed as the ratio of first charge capacity to first discharge capacity amounts to 73.3% for SNiS, 72.4% for SMoS2, 71.5% for Shybrid, and 83.6% for SNiS/MoS2. Such distinct capacity discrepancy in charge and discharge processes is caused by irreversible K+-consumption during initial formation of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. From the second to fifth cycle, the charge curves as well as discharge curves of SNiS almost stack together, but those for MoS2-bearing samples obviously do not. It seems that SNiS shows the best capacity retention. Nevertheless, the 5th-discharge capacity of SNiS is about 634 mAhg−1, which is much higher than its theoretical capacity (i.e. ∼ 591 mAhg−1). This over capacity is highly relevant to irreversible electrolyte decomposition and subsequent formation of an extra gel-like polymer film on NiS [48–50]. In other words, although SNiS exhibits relatively stable capacity in the first few cycles, it cannot sustain too long.

In view of this, long-term cycle performance of these cells is tested and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 4b. It is evident that NiS-bearing samples (i.e. SNiS and Shybrid) really do need more discharge–charge cycles to achieve capacity stability. After running for 1000 cycles, the discharge capacity follows the sequence: SNiS/MoS2 (472.1 mAhg−1) > SMoS2 (428.1 mAhg−1) > Shybrid (352.2 mAhg−1) > SNiS (296.4 mAhg−1). Based on these capacity values, one can easily estimate the weight ratio of NiS and MoS2 in Shybrid. The calculated result shows that Shybrid contains 57.6 wt% of NiS and 42.4 wt% of MoS2, being almost consistent with the pre-set mass ratio of 6:4 in sample preparation. However, similar calculation is not applicable for SNiS/MoS2, in that its capacity is well above those of SNiS and SMoS2. Put differently, there should be a significant synergistic effect between NiS and MoS2 phases in SNiS/MoS2. This synergy, as will be discussed in the following sections, greatly enhances K-storage capability, rendering SNiS/MoS2 among the top level of those reported previously (Table S1).

After long-term cycling, structural stability of electrode material is extremely important for its practical applications, and in this respect, microscopic characterizations are performed on 1000th-cycled SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid. Fig. 5 a shows particles of cycled SNiS/MoS2 seriously aggregate together with poorly defined boundaries. Their surfaces are made up of intertwined nanosheets that seem to be flexible and adhesive (Fig. 5b). Thankfully, their morphologies are uniform in the field of view, implying no phase separation during repetitious (de)potassiation. By contrast, the morphology of Shybrid changes a lot after long-term cycling. Figure S3 shows NiS particles can still remain spherical but with smoother surfaces as compared to those in Fig. S1b. Moreover, MoS2 phase also exhibits a spheroidal structure that is composed of blunt and adhesive nanosheets. In addition, some large structures with irregular shapes are also visible. These structural features resemble the reefs on the shore washed away by the waves. That means repeated (de)potassiation can polish the surface of SNiS and SMoS2 particles and cause structural collaspe and reorganization.

It is noteworthy that the distribution of MoS2 nanosheets in cycled SNiS/MoS2 differs significantly from that in pristine SNiS/MoS2. Fig. 5c and its close-up image (Fig. 5d) show the originally tiled nanosheets (Fig. 2d and e) expand outward like blooming flowers and even stand upright after long-term cycling. Most notably, the fringe spacing of MoS2 nanosheets increases from 1.84 Å (Fig. 2g) to 2.1 Å (Fig. 5e), revealing a change of exposed facets after repetitious (de)potassiation. This alteration gives better adaptation to the insertion and removal of large-sized K+ ions.

To reveal possible electrochemical reactions and their occurrence voltages during charge and discharge process, CV tests are conducted on fresh cells at a constant scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1 for five times in a row. Fig. 6 a shows the 1st CV curves of all cells look very different from subsequent ones. The most salient difference is the distinct cathodic peak centered on 0.5 V, which corresponds to the pseudo plateaux in the 1st discharge voltage curve (Fig. 4a). This peak is intimately related with irreversible decomposition of electrolyte and formation of SEI layer [51]. Among these 1st CV curves, the only immense discrepancy lies in the presence of an extra cathodic peak at 1.0 V for SNiS/MoS2. This peak is associated with insertion of K+ into NiS crystal [52]. From the second cycle onward, all cathodic and anodic peaks do not obviously widened, implying the absence of conspicuous pulverization and amorphization during cycling [53]. Moreover, the good superimposition of each set of CV curves reveals low polarizability and high structural stability of samples upon (de)potassiation [54]. An overview of these figures gives further demonstration that all curves consist of four cathodic peaks and three anodic peaks after 1st cycle. More than that, the peak voltages are well consistent with those of pseudo plateaux in voltage profiles (Fig. 4a). Considering a tremendous amount of acetylene black (20 wt%) is used in cell assembly, the sharp reversible cathodic peak below 0.1 V should be attributed to formation of K+-intercalated carbides [55]. Correspondingly, the anodic peak around 0.2 V should be assigned to extraction of K+ from graphite layers [56]. Moreover, the recurrent cathodic humps around 0.3 V are associated with reversible formation of SEI layers through reduction of electrolyte [51,57]. The broad peaks centered at 1.0 V in cathodic scan and 1.7 V in anodic scan are attributed to insertion and deinsertion of K+ in NiS crystal, respectively. More strikingly, the anodic peak at 2.4 V and cathodic peak at 1.9 V are highly relevant to reversible electrolyte decomposition, which is of great benefit to enhance K-storage capability of whole cell [58].

After the first five scans, successive CV tests are performed at 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 mV s−1 to disclose possible K-storage mechanisms. Fig. 6b shows voltage intervals between paired anodic/cathodic peaks gradually increase as increasing scan rate, suggesting kinetic limitation of K+ ions [59]. Besides, the current (i) also increases with scan rate (ν) in form of i = avb
[60], wherein a and b are constants to be determined. It is unanimously accepted that b = 0.5 suggests a diffusion-controlled K-storage mechanism and b = 1.0 reveals a capacitive one. By plotting the logarithmic relationship between anodic peak current and scan rate (Fig. S4), one can obtain b value from the slope of fitted line. The result shows that b values of SNiS, SMoS2, Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2 are 0.87, 0.91, 0.76 and 0.75, respectively. Unexceptionally, these values are within 0.5 ∼ 1.0, clearly manifesting coexistence of the above two mechanisms on K-storage. To quantitatively separate diffusive and capacitive contributions, i = avb is expanded to i = k 1ν + k 2ν1/2, wherein k 1ν and k 2ν1/2 refer to capacitive and diffusive contributions, respectively. By plotting and fitting i/ν1/2 vs. ν1/2 curves, one can obtain k 1 value at each voltage point V. As a result, an enclosed k 1ν vs. V curve at each scan rate is finally obtained. Thereafter, the capacitive contribution is determined as the integral area ratio of k 1ν vs. V and pristine CV curves, and thereby the diffusive one is gained by subtracting from 100%. Fig. 6c depicts histograms of capacitive and diffusive contributions, in which the former increases with the increase of scan rate while the latter shows an opposite trend. Notably, MoS2-bearing samples (i.e. SMoS2, Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2) provide over 50% capacitive contributions at any given scan rate, and these values of Shybrid and SNiS/MoS2 are apparently larger than those of SMoS2 and SNiS. This observation nicely confirms the combination of NiS and MoS2 phases can boost capacitive contribution to K-storage.

To seek interpretive insight into K-storage mechanism, ex situ XPS and FTIR characterizations are conducted to reveal surface element valence states and functional group types for SNiS/MoS2 at different electrochemical states, respectively. The XPS results are shown in Figs. 7-9
and the assignment of deconvoluted peaks is listed in Table 1 . In these figures, the full survey of XPS characterization (panel a) demonstrates the presence of C, O, Mo, Ni, S elements at each electrochemical state. Apparently, C and O elements in pristine SNiS/MoS2 are derived from atmospheric pollution during sample preparation, while those in 1st-discharged and 1st-charged SNiS/MoS2 are primarily stemmed from carbonate-based electrolyte. Notably, the full survey spectrum in Fig. 8a also presents distinct potassium signal after discharging SNiS/MoS2, corresponding to potassiation. This is because SNiS/MoS2 acts as cathode in half cells when potassium slice is used as reference electrode.

An overview of C1s core-level spectra in panel b of Figs. 7-9 gives a clear demonstration that C element has the same bonding mode of CC and CO at each electrochemical state. Except that the CC bond disappears in the discharge state, and with that comes CS, COK, COOK, K2p3/2, K2p1/2 and KPF6 signals. In the following charge process, CC bond reappears and K2p3/2, K2p1/2, KPF6 signals disappear due to depotassiation of the sample. From these observations, one can tentatively conclude that the SEI layer on SNiS/MoS2 may contain potassium alkanoate (RCOOK) and potassium oxalate (K2C2O4).

The high-resolution O1s spectra in panel c of Figs. 7-9 enable deep insights into the fundamental understanding of SEI composition. The common features of SNiS/MoS2 at each electrochemical state lie in the presence of C–OH hydroxylic groups, COC ether groups and CO3/H2O/O2 signals that can be attributed to the existence of loosely bound oxygen on sample surface. Beyond that, K2CO3 and KOH signals are present in both discharged and charged SNiS/MoS2, implying that they are the main inorganic components of SEI layer. Besides, COOH carboxyl groups are only present in discharge state, suggesting its being an important intermediate during potassiation.

The valence states of metallic elements are revealed in panels d and e of Figs. 7-9. From these figures, one can clearly distinguish Mo4+3d5/2, Mo4+3d3/2, Ni2+2p3/2, Ni2+2p1/2 signals and their satellite peaks at each electrochemical state. This observation strongly manifests the absence of reversible redox reactions during (de)potassiation. Besides, the peaks at 235 ∼ 236 eV are associated with Mo6+ of MoO3, revealing an irreversible oxidation of Mo4+ during sample preparation. Further, the high-resolution S2p core-level spectra present similar deconvoluted peaks that can be assigned to S2p3/2, S2p1/2, CS/SS and SO signals for all electrochemical states. The ever-present CS/SS and SO bonds imply some degree of amorphization upon cycling.

To validate the above XPS analysis and provide more information on SEI composition, FTIR experiments are performed and the results are shown in Fig. 10 . Common features of these spectra are their transmission peaks centered at 450 ∼ 590, 1210 ∼ 1270 and 3070 ∼ 3677 cm−1, which can be attributed to MoS/NiS/SS [63], COC in carboxylate groups [82] and OH stretching mode [83,84], respectively. As for pristine SNiS/MoS2, the peak located at 1631.4 cm−1 is assigned to CC, and the peak centered around 1099 cm−1 belongs to CO (Fig. 10a). After the first discharge and charge, their FTIR spectra bear many similarities (Fig. 10b and c). For instance, the bands of 2885 ∼ 2966 cm−1 are assigned to –CH2 vibration [85], and the peaks at 1107 and 1235 cm−1 correspond to CO oscillation [83,86–88]. Besides, the peaks centered around 1382, 1463, 1670, 1708, 1755 cm−1 indicate the existence of COO group [73,83].

Based on the aforementioned analyses, the composition of SEI layer may include but be not limited to C3H5O3K (i.e. CH3 CH2 OCOOK), C4H4O6K2 (i.e. KOCOOCH2 CH2 OCOOK), C2H5OK (i.e. KOCH2 CH3), C6H8O6K2 (i.e. KOCOOCH2 CH2 CH2 CH2 OCOOK), C2H4O2K2 (i.e. KOCH2 CH2 OK), K2CO3, K2C2O4 and KOH [66]. Upon potassiation, the following decomposition reactions of EC (C3H4O3) and DEC (C5H10O3) as well as parasitic reactions may happen.
(1) 2C3H4O3 + 2 K → C6H8O6K2
(2) C3H4O3 + 2 K + 2H → K2CO3 + C2H6(g)
(3) C3H4O3 + 2 K → C2H4O2K2 + CO(g)
(4) C3H4O3 + K + H → C2H5OK + CO2(g)
(5) C3H4O3 + 2 K → K2CO3 + C2H4(g)
(6) 2C3H4O3 + 2 K → C4H4O6K2 + C2H4(g)
(7) C5H10O3 + K + H → C3H5O3K + C2H6(g)
(8) C5H10O3 + 2 K → 2C2H5OK + CO(g)
(9) C6H8O6K2 + 2 K → K2C2O4 + C2H4O2K2 + C2H4(g)
(10) 2C3H5O3K + K + H → C2H5OK + K2C2O4 + C2H6O(l)
(11) C4H4O6K2 + 2 K → C2H4O2K2 + K2C2O4
(12) C3H5O3K + K + H → K2CO3 + C2H6(g)
(13) C5H10O3 + C2H5OK → C3H5O3K + C4H10O(g)
Further evidence should be provided by combining gas chromatography (GC)-mass spectrometry and GC/FTIR in future research.

The activation of sulfur atoms on the basal plane of 1T-MoS2 not only improves cycle performance as demonstrated above but also enhances rate performance. To validate the latter point, cells are electrochemically cycled at 0.2 A/g for 200 times to achieve capacity stabilization before successively cycling at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 5 and 0.2 A/g. Fig. 11 a shows SNiS/MoS2 exhibits much stronger K-storage capability than Shybrid at each given current density. It delivers 532.8 mAh/g in the 5th cycle at 0.2 A/g, which drops off to 273.1 mAh/g at 5 A/g with a capacity loss of ∼ 48.7%. Comparatively, Shybrid merely gives 420.8 mAh/g in the 5th cycle at 0.2 A/g, and its capacity loss at 5 A/g amounts to 65%. When back to 0.2 A/g, the discharge capacities of SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid swiftly rise with decent recovery of 97.8% and 96.3%, respectively. Apparently, the rate performance of SNiS/MoS2 is much superior to that of Shybrid, and such pronounced discrepancy cannot be understood by simply comparing their capacitive contributions. More often than not, capacitive contribution is provided by surface adsorption of desolvated K+ ions from electrolyte, which is fairly fast and highly reversible. Along this line, the more capacitive contribution the sample provides the better rate performance it exhibits [89]. This, however, is not the case in our study. As illustrated in Fig. 6c, capacitive contributions of SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid are nearly identical, but their rate capabilities are quite disparate. In other words, comparing capacitive contributions alone is unable to accurately reveal disparities in rate performance. Even if the capacitive contributions are the same, their different contributing manners can still lead to great performance discrepancy. Thereby, the synergy between NiS and MoS2 phases is supposed to be predominantly responsible for the exceptional rate capability of SNiS/MoS2. Fig. 11b depicts selected voltage curves of SNiS/MoS2 at any given current density. It is evident that the curve shape does not essentially change, which means SNiS/MoS2 indeed has high electrochemical reaction rate and low polarization resistance.

To further reveal impedance change of SNiS/MoS2 during rate-performance test, EIS measurements are carried out and the Nyquist plots are portrayed in Fig. 11c. It is apparent that each plot consists of a semicircle at high frequencies and a straight line at low frequencies. To fit these plots, an equivalent circuit is modeled and depicted in the inset of Fig. 11c. It includes an ohmic resistance of electrolyte and cell components (R e), an intercalation capacitance (C), a charge-transfer resistance at the interface of electrode/electrolyte (R ct), a constant phase element (CPEsf, with ‘sf’ denoting surface film) and a Warburg impedance (W) [90,91]. After iterative fitting, the obtained R ct value increases firstly and then decreases (inset of Fig. 11c). It shows an upward trend from 124.7 Ω at 0.2 A/g to 168.9 Ω at 0.5 A/g, then to 185.3 Ω at 1 A/g, and finally to 274.9 Ω at 5 A/g. Such impedance rise is intimately associated with diffusion limitations [49]. At high current densities, (de)potassiation process is very fast and the resultant large strain makes SNiS/MoS2 easily lose its close contact with conductive agent, thereby increasing internal resistance. When the current density is back to 0.2 A/g, the R ct value descends to 175.4 Ω. This impedance drop revives K-storage capability of SNiS/MoS2, and hence yielding decent capacity retention as observed in Fig. 11a.
Based on above electrochemical discussions, following findings may be helpful to figure out the synergy between NiS and MoS2 phases, and thereby inferring possible K-storage mechanisms in SNiS/MoS2.
(i) The K-storage capacity of SNiS/MoS2 is much above those of SNiS, SMoS2 and Shybrid.
(ii) When storing K+ ions in SNiS/MoS2, the capacitive contribution outperforms diffusive one at any given scan rate.
(iii) TEM and HRTEM results confirm structural integrity of SNiS/MoS2 after long-term cycling.
(iv) CV tests illustrate the presence of (de)insertion of K+ in NiS phase.

(v) XPS results show unchanged valence states of metal elements during charge and discharge, but nevertheless demonstrate the emergence of various sulfur-bearing and carbon-bearing functional groups upon cycling.
(vi) Current responses in CV curves for SNiS/MoS2 are generally lower than those for SNiS, SMoS2 and Shybrid.

In the light of these findings, one can schematically depict electrode structure of SNiS/MoS2 (Fig. 12 a). The sketch map of Shybrid electrode is also portrayed as a reference (Fig. 12b). As is known, the electrical double-layer (EDL) energy storage is always available when desolvated K+ ions are physically adsorbed into MoS2 interlayers. This adsorption process is fast, reversible and non-Faradaic, which avoids any significant structural variation and thereby allows for high-power capability and cycling stability. Evidently, the primary limiting factor for the EDL effect is surface area. The larger the surface area, the more obvious the EDL effect. Given that SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid are pretty close in terms of BET surface area, therefore they should possess nearly identical EDL capacity even though their MoS2 components exist in different geometric shapes. To be specific, the EDL capacity values in SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid should be directly proportional to 4πε 0 R 1 R 2/(R 2-R 1) and ε0 S/d, respectively. Here, ε 0 is the permittivity of vacuum, R 1 and R 2 are curvature radii of neighboring MoS2 lamellae in SNiS/MoS2, S and d denote surface area and layer spacing of MoS2 lamellae in Shybrid, respectively. It is obvious that R 2-R 1 can be taken as d and 4πR 1 R 2 is approximated to the area of spherical surface (i.e. S) when R 1 and R 2 are close enough. In this scenario, the EDL capacity of MoS2 phase should be identical in SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid. Accordingly, the distribution of NiS phase is predominantly responsible for the capacity discrepancy between SNiS/MoS2 and Shybrid. In Fig. 12a, the NiS phase is encapsulated by MoS2 lamellae, forming a concentric spherical capacitor that provides additional large K-storage capacity. By contrast, the NiS phase in Fig. 12b are detached from MoS2 lamellae, forming an insertion electrode unit in which K+ ions have to undergo rather sluggish solid-state diffusion. Moreover, preceding analyses on XPS and voltage profiles confirm the absence of redox reactions and thereby exclude the possibility of bond breakage as well as significant structural changes beyond the inclusion or removal of K+ ions. Accordingly, the NiS phase in Shybrid is unable to cooperate with MoS2 phase to store K+ ions, so that the K-storage capacity of Shybrid can be facilely estimated on the basis of the weight ratio and individual capacity of NiS and MoS2 phases. In stark contrast, the NiS phase in SNiS/MoS2 plays two important roles. The first is to directly store K+ ions through slow solid-state (de)insertion, which is the same as what happens in Shybrid. The second is to function as a dielectric material that constitutes spherical capacitors to greatly boost K-storage capability of MoS2 lamellae. At the effect of NiS phase, SNiS/MoS2 should become more capacitive upon cycling because (i) most K-storage modes based on irreversible reactions (e.g. electrolyte decomposition) are greatly suppressed after long-term charge and discharge; (ii) charge donation that refers to K+-induced chemical exfoliation can trigger the transition from 2H to 1T phase in MoS2, which enhances the conductivity of electrode material. Fig. 12c shows the capacitive contributions of 1000th-cycled SNiS/MoS2 are apparently larger than those of the pristine one at any given scan rate (Fig. 6c). The capacitance CV curves, depicted as boundaries of the pink parts in Fig. 12d, encompass most of the area in the original CV curves, clearly manifesting the leading role of capacitive mechanism. For this reason, SNiS/MoS2 presents the top cycling capacity and the most exceptional rate performance. However, this structural design has an obvious shortcoming that its conductivity is relatively low as compared to SNiS, SMoS2 and Shybrid. This will result in lower current response of SNiS/MoS2, as observed in Fig. 6a and b. The underlying reason is that the conductive agent (i.e. acetylene black) cannot form close contact with the semi-conductive NiS phase (E g ≈ 0.4 eV) in SNiS/MoS2, weakening electrical connectivity among active particles.

In summary, a two-step route is proposed and adopted to in situ grow 1T-MoS2 nanosheets on NiS porous-hollow microspheres. The resultant structure, resembling flower bud in bud, presents desired surface contact between NiS and 1T-MoS2 phases, which can effectively activate inert sulfur atoms on the basal plane of the latter. This structure delivers a high K-storage capacity of 472.1 mAhg−1 after the 1000th discharge at 0.2 A/g with an ICE of 83.6%. After such long-term cycling, it can still maintain structural integrity with no trace of phase separation. The rate performance test also demonstrates its high capacity of 273.1 mAh/g at 5 A/g, which is more than half of the value that is measured at 0.2 A/g. The K-storage mechanisms in this unique structure can be classified into two categories: diffusive process and EDL regime. It is found that NiS phase mainly acts as a dielectric material to boost K-storage capability of the cladding MoS2 lamellae, and this EDL effect is more prominent either at high current densities or upon long-term cycling. On the other hand, CV test confirms the presence of reversible electrolyte decomposition at high voltages, and the decomposition products may well include C3H5O3K, C4H4O6K2, C2H5OK, C6H8O6K2, C2H4O2K2, K2CO3, K2C2O4 and KOH. Such electrolyte-relevant reactions that involve reversible formation and decomposition of organic polymeric substances may also contribute to K-storage capacity of the whole cell.
